content.text: The sequence in which demonstration examples are presented to a language model
shapes its predictions far more than most practitioners expect — the same set of
examples arranged differently can shift classification accuracy by ten percentage points
or more, introduce systematic bias toward the last-shown class, and alter the output
format the model chooses to follow.
What it is
In few-shot prompting, a handful of
demonstration examples
— input–output pairs drawn from a labelled pool — are prepended to the user's query
before it is sent to the model.
Example ordering
refers to the deliberate choice (or accidental default) of
which position each example occupies within that prefix.
Language models process tokens sequentially, and their attention mechanism assigns
position-dependent weight to context. Examples placed near the end of the prompt
— closest to the query — receive disproportionately high attention in the model's
forward pass (the
recency effect
). When demonstration labels cluster in a particular position, the model
systematically mirrors that cluster in its output — even when the underlying data
distribution is balanced.
Example ordering is one of three primary axes of few-shot design, alongside
example selection
(which examples to include) and
example count
(how many shots to use). These axes interact: a well-selected pool arranged in the
wrong order can perform worse than a randomly-selected pool with good ordering.
Why it matters
In agentic systems, a single few-shot classifier or router can gate the entire
downstream pipeline. A router that categorises a user query as
billing
vs
support
vs
sales
and hands off to the appropriate sub-agent has its accuracy determined largely by
the structure of its few-shot prompt — not just the quality of its training.
The practical danger is that label bias from poor ordering is
silent
: the model confidently produces a plausible-looking label. Without measuring
accuracy across a held-out evaluation set — or without running ablations over
multiple orderings — the team never discovers that the prompt is systematically
mislabelling 20 % of a minority class.
Empirical studies on GPT-3 and its successors (e.g. Zhao et al., 2021; Lu et al., 2022)
documented variance as high as 30 percentage-points in accuracy across different
orderings of the same pool of examples. More recent instruction-tuned models are
somewhat more robust, but the effect persists — especially for classification tasks
and structured generation where the output format is learned from the last example seen.
Benefits
Applying a deliberate ordering strategy, rather than relying on the default insertion
order of an example database, delivers the following concrete improvements.
Reduced classification bias.
Balanced and interleaved orderings prevent any single label from dominating the recency window, leading to more uniform per-class F1 scores.
More stable zero-variance inference.

When the same query is processed multiple times (e.g. for consistency checks), a fixed canonical order produces deterministic output. A seeded shuffle provides reproducibility while still decorrelating from pool insertion order.
Better format fidelity.

The last example's output format is the strongest template for the model. By controlling which example is last, authors can enforce a desired output schema (e.g., JSON, a specific regex pattern) with minimal instruction overhead.
Improved few-shot efficiency.
A good ordering with k=4 examples can match the accuracy of a naive ordering with k=8 examples, halving token cost and latency.
Fairer evaluation benchmarks.
Shuffling or averaging over multiple orderings eliminates order-induced variance from benchmark scores, producing more reliable capability measurements.
Trade-offs
Deliberate ordering is not free of cost or complexity.
Increased prompt engineering surface area.
Teams must now reason about a third axis of few-shot design. This extends evaluation time and can create confusion between ordering effects and selection effects when debugging accuracy regressions.
Seed management overhead.
Seeded shuffles require storing and versioning seeds in production config. A seed change between deployments can silently change model behaviour.
Proximity-based strategies need embeddings.
Ordering examples by semantic similarity to the query requires an embedding lookup on every inference call, adding latency and a vector store dependency.
Context window pressure.
Strategies that append many shots to hit a balanced label set can grow the prompt beyond the target context budget, increasing cost or forcing truncation.
Diminishing returns on large k.

With many examples, the recency effect narrows: the model effectively averages over a longer context and individual position matters less. Ordering strategies yield the most value for k in the range 3–8.
How it works
Recency bias and attention weighting
Transformer-based models process context via self-attention. Each token attends to
all prior tokens with weights given by the softmax of scaled dot-products. For
in-context examples, the query token at position
assigns attention weight
to example-token at position
. In practice, the distribution
is skewed: the recency effect means that tokens at positions
systematically receive higher weight.
The label in the final example before the query therefore functions as a high-weight
prior in the model's implicit posterior. If all negative examples appear in positions
k-2 and k-1 and the query is ambiguous, the model's prediction distribution shifts
toward 'negative' not because the evidence supports it but because the context
window primes it.
Label imbalance in position space
Even when the pool is globally balanced (equal counts per class), a fixed ordering
can create local imbalance in the recency window. Denote the k demonstration
examples as
in prompt order. Define the
recency window
as the last
examples
. If the fraction of examples with label
in the window differs from the global fraction:
then the model is exposed to a position-biased view of the label distribution,
and its output will reflect that bias. Balanced interleaving minimises this gap by
distributing each class uniformly across all positions.
Easy-to-hard curriculum
A separate ordering intuition comes from curriculum learning: placing simpler,
prototypical examples early and harder, edge-case examples late allows the model
to 'build' its in-context representation incrementally. The model first anchors on
the clearest exemplars of each class, then refines against harder cases. This tends
to help on tasks where the decision boundary is complex (e.g., multi-label sentiment,
intent classification with overlapping categories).
Proximity-based ordering
For retrieval-augmented few-shot prompts, the most semantically similar examples
are intentionally placed last. This exploits recency bias constructively: the model
reads its closest analogues immediately before the query, making the analogy transfer
as direct as possible. This strategy is popularised by dense retrievers like FAISS
combined with Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) selection to simultaneously maximise
similarity and maintain diversity.
Architecture
The diagram below shows how a shot pool passes through an ordering strategy block
before being assembled into a prompt. Three parallel paths illustrate the outcomes
of balanced, shuffled, and recency-biased orderings.

attrs.alt: Example ordering pipeline showing shot pool, strategy selector, three ordering paths, LLM, and prediction outputs

content.text: The key insight illustrated above: the same LLM receives the same examples but in
different positions. The recency-biased path (right column) exposes the model to a
cluster of one class immediately before the query, producing a skewed prediction.
The balanced path (centre) eliminates that local imbalance.
When to use it
Use deliberate ordering when
The task involves
multi-class or binary classification
— label distribution in the prompt directly affects output distribution.
The few-shot pool is
imbalanced
(more examples of one class), making random ordering even riskier.
The agent runs in production with a
fixed prompt
— a one-time ordering decision locks in any bias for every inference.
The team runs
benchmark evaluations
and wants scores that are comparable across runs and models.
The task requires a
specific output schema
and the last example is used as the format template.
When ordering matters less
Very large k (≥ 20 shots): recency effects average out over many examples.
Tasks where the system prompt provides an explicit instruction ("always output JSON"): format is anchored by instruction, not mimicry.
Models with extremely strong instruction-following training (GPT-4-class models on well-specified tasks): still worth testing, but impact is smaller.
Open-ended generation tasks with no single correct label: ordering affects style more than accuracy, and style variation may be acceptable.
How to implement
The following steps provide a complete implementation path for production few-shot classifiers with principled example ordering.
Audit your pool.
Count examples per class. If any class is under-represented by more than 2×, augment the pool before applying ordering — ordering cannot compensate for fundamental data scarcity.
Choose a strategy.
Default to balanced interleaving for classification. Add proximity sorting only if you have an embedding pipeline already in place.
Implement with a fixed seed.
Use a deterministic seed derived from the query hash for reproducibility across deployments. Store the seed-generation function in version control.
Evaluate across multiple orderings.
On your held-out set, run at least 5 independent random shuffles and report mean ± std accuracy. If std > 3 %, switch to balanced interleaving.
Place the live query last.
Always put the query at the very end of the prompt, after a clear delimiter (e.g., a blank line and the label prefix). Never interleave the query within examples.
Pin your prompt version.
Treat the ordered prompt template as a versioned artefact. Log the ordering strategy and seed alongside every inference call for post-hoc debugging.
Examples
Example 1 — Comparing ordering strategies in Python
This runnable script demonstrates three orderings — recency-biased, balanced, and randomly shuffled — and prints the resulting prompt for visual inspection.

import random

# Simulate measuring recency bias by swapping example order
# in a few-shot classification prompt.

def build_prompt(examples, query):
    """Build a few-shot prompt given ordered (text, label) pairs."""
    lines = []
    for text, label in examples:
        lines.append(f"Input: {text}")
        lines.append(f"Label: {label}")
    lines.append(f"Input: {query}")
    lines.append("Label:")
    return "\n".join(lines)

# Pool of labelled examples
pool = [
    ("The product arrived broken.",  "negative"),
    ("Fast shipping, great quality!", "positive"),
    ("Terrible customer support.",   "negative"),
    ("Works exactly as described.",   "positive"),
    ("Fell apart after one day.",     "negative"),
    ("Best purchase I've made.",      "positive"),
]

query = "Nothing spectacular but does the job."

# ── Strategy 1: all negatives last (recency-biased) ──
biased_order = sorted(pool, key=lambda x: x[1])  # positives first, negatives last
print("=== Biased order (negatives last) ===")
print(build_prompt(biased_order, query))
print()

# ── Strategy 2: balanced alternation ──
pos = [e for e in pool if e[1] == "positive"]
neg = [e for e in pool if e[1] == "negative"]
balanced = [x for pair in zip(pos, neg) for x in pair]
print("=== Balanced alternation ===")
print(build_prompt(balanced, query))
print()

# ── Strategy 3: random shuffle (repeat 3 times to show variance) ──
print("=== Random shuffles ===")
for i in range(3):
    shuffled = pool[:]
    random.shuffle(shuffled)
    print(f"Run {i+1}: {[label for _, label in shuffled]}")
Example 2 — Production-grade stratified shot sampler
A typed, extensible sampler supporting four strategies. Suitable for use inside an agentic pipeline that builds prompts at inference time.

import random
import itertools
from typing import Literal

ExampleLabel = str
Example = tuple[str, ExampleLabel]

def stratified_shot_sampler(
    pool: list[Example],
    k: int,
    strategy: Literal["balanced", "random", "easy_hard", "proximity"] = "balanced",
    query: str = "",
    seed: int | None = None,
) -> list[Example]:
    """
    Select k examples from pool using the specified ordering strategy.

    balanced  — equal class representation, interleaved by label.
    random    — uniform random sample; use a fixed seed for reproducibility.
    easy_hard — shorter examples first (proxy for 'easy'), longer last.
    proximity — examples whose text overlaps most with query come last
                (high recency weight for the most relevant examples).
    """
    rng = random.Random(seed)

    if strategy == "random":
        return rng.sample(pool, min(k, len(pool)))

    if strategy == "balanced":
        by_label: dict[str, list[Example]] = {}
        for ex in pool:
            by_label.setdefault(ex[1], []).append(ex)
        # Shuffle within each class
        for cls in by_label:
            rng.shuffle(by_label[cls])
        # Round-robin interleave
        classes = list(by_label.values())
        interleaved = list(itertools.islice(
            (ex for ex in itertools.chain.from_iterable(
                itertools.zip_longest(*classes)
            ) if ex is not None),
            k,
        ))
        return interleaved

    if strategy == "easy_hard":
        sorted_pool = sorted(pool, key=lambda e: len(e[0].split()))
        return sorted_pool[:k]

    if strategy == "proximity":
        query_tokens = set(query.lower().split())
        def overlap(ex):
            return len(set(ex[0].lower().split()) & query_tokens)
        sorted_pool = sorted(pool, key=overlap)   # least relevant first
        selected = sorted_pool[-k:]               # most relevant closest to query
        return selected

    raise ValueError(f"Unknown strategy: {strategy}")


# ── Demo ──
pool = [
    ("I love this product!",        "positive"),
    ("Completely useless.",          "negative"),
    ("Great value for the price.",   "positive"),
    ("Stopped working in a week.",   "negative"),
    ("Highly recommend to everyone.","positive"),
    ("Waste of money.",              "negative"),
    ("Exceeded my expectations.",    "positive"),
    ("Very poor build quality.",     "negative"),
]

query = "It does the job, nothing more."

for strat in ("random", "balanced", "easy_hard", "proximity"):
    selected = stratified_shot_sampler(pool, k=4, strategy=strat, query=query, seed=42)
    print(f"[{strat:12s}] {[label for _, label in selected]}")
Real-world example

A SaaS company operates a customer-support agent that receives 50,000 queries per day. An LLM-based router decides whether to send each query to the billing_agent, auth_agent, or shipping_agent. The router uses a fixed 6-shot prompt drawn from a pool of 27 labelled examples.

Initial deployment used insertion order from the annotation database, which happened to place all shipping examples last (the annotators had worked through categories sequentially). The router misclassified 18 % of auth queries as shipping. After switching to seeded balanced interleaving, the per-class error rate dropped below 4 % across all three routes — with no change to the example pool, the model, or any other prompt component.

"""
Production few-shot router: routes user queries to specialist agents.
Uses balanced interleaving + seeded shuffle for deterministic evals.
"""
import hashlib, random, itertools
from typing import Any

ROUTING_POOL = [
    {"query": "How do I reset my password?",          "route": "auth_agent"},
    {"query": "My payment failed at checkout.",        "route": "billing_agent"},
    {"query": "Can I get a refund?",                   "route": "billing_agent"},
    {"query": "I can't log into my account.",          "route": "auth_agent"},
    {"query": "Where is my order?",                    "route": "shipping_agent"},
    {"query": "Track my shipment #12345.",             "route": "shipping_agent"},
    {"query": "Two-factor auth keeps failing.",        "route": "auth_agent"},
    {"query": "I was charged twice.",                  "route": "billing_agent"},
    {"query": "Estimated delivery date?",              "route": "shipping_agent"},
]

def seeded_balanced_sample(pool: list[dict], k: int, seed_str: str) -> list[dict]:
    """Reproducible balanced sample — same query always gets same ordering."""
    seed = int(hashlib.md5(seed_str.encode()).hexdigest(), 16) % (2**32)
    rng = random.Random(seed)

    by_route: dict[str, list[dict]] = {}
    for ex in pool:
        by_route.setdefault(ex["route"], []).append(ex)

    for cls in by_route:
        rng.shuffle(by_route[cls])

    queues = list(by_route.values())
    interleaved = list(itertools.islice(
        (ex for ex in itertools.chain.from_iterable(itertools.zip_longest(*queues)) if ex),
        k,
    ))
    return interleaved

def build_routing_prompt(examples: list[dict], query: str) -> str:
    lines = ["Route the user query to the correct agent.\n"]
    for ex in examples:
        lines.append(f"Query: {ex['query']}")
        lines.append(f"Agent: {ex['route']}\n")
    lines.append(f"Query: {query}")
    lines.append("Agent:")
    return "\n".join(lines)

# ── Use in production ──
user_query = "My subscription renewal keeps failing."
shots = seeded_balanced_sample(ROUTING_POOL, k=6, seed_str=user_query)
prompt = build_routing_prompt(shots, user_query)
print(prompt)
# A well-ordered prompt ensures billing_agent and auth_agent both appear
# in the last few positions, preventing the model from biasing toward shipping.

The seeded hash approach — deriving the shuffle seed from a hash of the query — ensures that identical queries always receive identical prompts, making the system fully deterministic while still breaking the correlation between pool insertion order and prompt position.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — Using insertion order as default

The most pervasive mistake is assuming that because examples are labelled correctly, their order doesn't matter. Databases often reflect annotation session order, which clusters labels by class. The prompt below silently biases the model toward 'negative'.

# ✗ WRONG — fixed order, all negatives last
examples = [
    ("Great product!", "positive"),
    ("Love it!",       "positive"),
    ("Brilliant!",     "positive"),
    ("Broke on day 1.","negative"),
    ("Terrible.",      "negative"),
    ("Waste of money.","negative"),
]
# The model will strongly favour the 'negative' label
# because it's the recency-dominant class in the prompt.

# ✓ RIGHT — balanced interleave, seeded shuffle
import random

def balanced_interleave(pool, seed=42):
    rng = random.Random(seed)
    pos = [e for e in pool if e[1] == "positive"]
    neg = [e for e in pool if e[1] == "negative"]
    rng.shuffle(pos); rng.shuffle(neg)
    # zip interleaves: pos[0], neg[0], pos[1], neg[1], ...
    return [x for pair in zip(pos, neg) for x in pair]

examples = [
    ("Great product!", "positive"),
    ("Love it!",       "positive"),
    ("Brilliant!",     "positive"),
    ("Broke on day 1.","negative"),
    ("Terrible.",      "negative"),
    ("Waste of money.","negative"),
]
ordered = balanced_interleave(examples)
print([label for _, label in ordered])
# ['positive', 'negative', 'positive', 'negative', 'positive', 'negative']
Mistake 2 — Not evaluating order variance

Teams often report a single accuracy number from a single ordering. The correct procedure is to report accuracy as a distribution over at least five independent random shuffles of the pool. High variance indicates that ordering is the dominant performance driver and that a deterministic strategy is urgently needed.
Mistake 3 — Mixing the query into the example block

Inserting the live query anywhere other than the final position — for example, to 'balance' the prompt by inserting it mid-sequence — destroys the few-shot pattern. The model expects to predict the output of the item directly following the last example; any deviation forces it to generalise from a broken template.
Mistake 4 — Changing seeds between train and eval

If the prompt ordering used during evaluation differs from the ordering used in production inference (e.g., because the team added a new example to the pool), the benchmark score may not reflect production accuracy. Always evaluate on the exact prompt — including ordering seed — that will run in production.
Best practices
Default to balanced interleaving.
For classification tasks with up to 6 shots, balanced interleaving by label consistently outperforms other strategies with no additional infrastructure cost.
Seed every shuffle deterministically.
Derive the seed from a hash of the query or a version-controlled constant. Never rely on the system clock or an unseeded random.Random() instance.
Place the most format-representative example last.
If the output must follow a strict schema, ensure the last demonstration is a clean, well-formatted example of the target schema.
Run an ordering ablation before shipping.
Test at least three strategies — random, balanced, proximity — on 200+ held-out examples. Pick the strategy with highest mean accuracy and lowest std deviation.
Version and log prompts.
Treat the full prompt (including ordered examples and seeds) as a versioned artefact. Log the prompt hash alongside every inference for debugging and auditability.
Separate pool updates from ordering updates.
Adding a new example to the pool is a schema-breaking change. Decouple pool version from ordering seed to reason about each independently.
Avoid pooling across domains.
Examples from different input distributions should not be mixed in the same shot pool. Domain-mixed pools create orderings that are hard to interpret and may degrade accuracy for all classes.
Performance & scalability
Example ordering directly affects prompt length, which in turn affects cost and latency for token-priced APIs.
Token budget
Each additional shot in the prompt adds proportional tokens. For a prompt with k shots, each of average length
tokens, the total prompt overhead is:

Strategies that require k > 6 to achieve class balance in the window (e.g., because the pool has 5 classes) can double or triple the prompt token count. Measure and cap k: for 5 classes with 1 shot each, the recency window is only 1 example per class, so increasing to 2 shots per class (k=10) gives more stability. Beyond k=10, returns diminish quickly while cost grows linearly.
Caching and prompt reuse

If the shot prefix is fixed (same examples in the same order for all queries in a session), LLM providers that support KV-cache prefix reuse (e.g., Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI prefix caching) will amortise the cost of the example block across all queries in the batch. This makes a fixed balanced ordering significantly cheaper at scale than proximity-sorted orderings that change per query.

Proximity sorting defeats prefix caching because each query produces a different example sequence. Weigh the accuracy gain from proximity sorting against the latency and cost increase from cache misses. Empirically, balanced interleaving with MMR selection achieves 80–90 % of the accuracy gain of full proximity ordering while being compatible with prefix caching.
Tooling & ecosystem
LangChain ExampleSelectors
LangChain provides ExampleSelector classes that combine selection and ordering.
SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector
returns examples sorted by embedding distance to the query (closest last — exploiting recency).
MaxMarginalRelevanceExampleSelector
adds diversity: it selects a semantically diverse subset and still places the most similar example last.

from langchain.prompts import FewShotPromptTemplate, PromptTemplate
from langchain.prompts.example_selector import (
    SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector,
    MaxMarginalRelevanceExampleSelector,
)
from langchain_community.vectorstores import FAISS
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

examples = [
    {"input": "I love this!", "output": "positive"},
    {"input": "Terrible product.", "output": "negative"},
    {"input": "Exceeded my expectations.", "output": "positive"},
    {"input": "Broke after a week.", "output": "negative"},
]

# Semantic similarity — most-relevant shots come last (high recency weight)
selector = SemanticSimilarityExampleSelector.from_examples(
    examples,
    OpenAIEmbeddings(),
    FAISS,
    k=3,
)

example_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["input", "output"],
    template="Input: {input}\nOutput: {output}",
)

prompt = FewShotPromptTemplate(
    example_selector=selector,
    example_prompt=example_prompt,
    suffix="Input: {query}\nOutput:",
    input_variables=["query"],
)

print(prompt.format(query="Good value but nothing special."))
# Selected examples are ordered by semantic distance to query;
# the closest example appears last, leveraging recency attention.
DSPy

DSPy's LM module and Signature framework compile few-shot examples programmatically. When using a bootstrapped few-shot teleprompter, DSPy sorts and selects examples from the training set automatically. Custom orderings can be injected by overriding the module's
demos
attribute with a pre-sorted list before calling
module.forward()
Promptfoo

The open-source evaluation framework Promptfoo supports prompt variants. By defining multiple prompt templates that differ only in example order, an ablation study can be run with a single YAML config, collecting accuracy, cost, and latency metrics across all orderings in one pipeline run.
FAISS and Pinecone

For proximity-based ordering in high-throughput systems, FAISS (local) and Pinecone (managed) provide approximate nearest-neighbour search over example embeddings. The query is embedded at inference time, and the k nearest examples are returned already ranked by distance — the last item in the ranked list (most similar) is placed closest to the query in the prompt.
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares the main example-ordering strategies across key dimensions.
Strategy
Recency Bias Control
Needs Embeddings

Best For

No
No

No

No

Yes

Calibration-based
Strong — corrected post-hoc
No
Yes
When modifying the prompt is impractical

Calibration-based approaches (e.g., contextual calibration, domain-conditional calibration) post-process model output probabilities to remove label bias induced by ordering, without changing the prompt itself. They are powerful when the prompt is read-only but add inference overhead (multiple forward passes to estimate bias).
Security & reliability
Prompt injection via example ordering

In retrieval-augmented few-shot systems, the example pool is populated dynamically from user-submitted content or a third-party database. An adversary can craft inputs that, when retrieved as examples, occupy the last position in the prompt and override the intended output format. This is a form of prompt injection mediated by the ordering strategy.

Mitigation: sanitise retrieved examples through a content filter before insertion. In balanced interleaving, the last example is drawn deterministically by class rather than by similarity score, limiting an adversary's ability to guarantee their content appears last.
Auditability and reproducibility

Agentic systems that make consequential decisions (e.g., fraud flagging, content moderation) must be auditable. If the example order changes between inference calls — due to pool updates, seed drift, or retrieval non-determinism — the same input can produce different outputs at different times, making post-hoc debugging impossible.

Reliability practices: log the exact prompt (or a cryptographic hash of it) for every inference call. Pin the example pool version with a semantic version tag. Alert on any change to the pool or seed that would alter the resulting prompt for a canonical test query.
Failure modes
Pool corruption:
a mis-labelled example inserted into the pool and positioned last will strongly bias predictions toward the wrong class until caught.
Seed collision:
two different queries that hash to the same seed produce identical orderings; in adversarial settings, an attacker can craft queries that replay a known-bad ordering.
Context window overflow:

if the balanced strategy requires more shots than fit in the context window, a naive truncation will silently drop examples from the start — often cutting the 'easy' prototypical examples and leaving only hard edge cases.
Key takeaways
The order of few-shot examples is not cosmetic: it can shift classification accuracy by 10–30 percentage points on the same example pool due to the recency effect in transformer attention.

Balanced label interleaving is the safest default for classification and routing tasks — it distributes each class uniformly across prompt positions, eliminating local label imbalance in the recency window.
Always use a deterministic, version-controlled seed for shuffles in production; derive it from a hash of the query or a pinned constant so the prompt is reproducible and auditable.
Placing the most format-representative example last leverages recency bias constructively to enforce output schema without additional instruction tokens.
Proximity-sorted orderings (most-similar example last) improve accuracy on retrieval-augmented tasks but defeat LLM prefix caching, increasing cost and latency at scale.
Evaluate ordering variance by running at least five independent shuffles on a held-out set before shipping; if accuracy std > 3 %, switch to a deterministic balanced strategy.

In dynamic retrieval pipelines, treat example ordering as a security surface: adversarially crafted retrieved content can occupy the last position and inject instructions; sanitise and pin retrieved examples.
Log the full prompt or its hash alongside every inference call so that ordering-induced regressions can be reproduced and root-caused after deployment.
